topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammering started before the sun had fully cleared the horizon. I stood by my window, my hands gripping the sill so tightly my knuckles were white. Every rhythmic *thwack* against the wood felt like a physical assault on my senses. When Mr. Henderson finally emerged, whistling a tuneless melody, his grin was wide and entirely too pleased with himself. "Morning, Martha! Just getting this monstrosity up," he announced, gesturing toward the freshly erected, aggressively tall wooden barrier. I didn't move. I just stared at the way the new planks seemed to mock the neat line of my own property. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a heavy, unpleasant pressure. I could feel the urge to retreat, to somehow erase the sight of it from my vision.

[story 2]

I spent the entire afternoon pacing the length of my yard, my breath coming in shallow, rapid bursts. The sheer volume of the construction—the screech of the saw, the clumsy placement of the posts—was grating on my nerves like sandpaper. When Henderson stopped for a break, wiping sweat from his brow with a greasy rag, he clapped me heartily on the shoulder. "Isn't it nice, Martha? A little privacy." His voice was too cheerful, too buoyant. I flinched away from the touch, pulling my arm back stiffly. I kept my eyes fixed on the fence line, tracing the crude vertical lines with my gaze, feeling a profound sense of wrongness settle deep in my chest, making my throat feel suddenly dry and constricted.

[story 3]

I found myself standing at the edge of my property line, my feet rooted to the patchy lawn. The new fence was a jarring intrusion, a sharp, angular interruption to the familiar view. I watched Henderson meticulously aligning the bottom rail, his movements precise yet utterly lacking in consideration for the existing landscape. A wave of intense unease washed over me, making my skin prickle. "It’s going to look so tidy," he commented, puffing out his chest. I managed a small, tight nod, my jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I wanted to shout, to demand he stop, but the words caught, lodged behind a sudden, unwelcome wall of stillness.
